grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Mirza Ghalib's ghazal titled *Aah ko chahiye ik umr asar hote tak*, the opening couplet states that a sigh (*aah*) requires an entire lifetime (*ik umr*, rendered here as "an age") to take effect or have an impact (*asar hote tak*, "to take its flight").[^1]

This sets the theme of profound, enduring longing in the beloved's tangled locks (*zulf*), where even a sigh's influence is delayed across a human lifespan.

cases.prompt: Which couplet in Ghalib's 'Koi ummeed bar nahin aati' is most famous for the line about hopelessness?
cases.rationale: Should land on chapter 115 of Diwan-e-Ghalib and quote the matla.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The most famous couplet for its line about hopelessness is the opening sher (couplet) itself, which gives the ghazal its title and sets the theme of dashed expectations:**

> Koi ummeed bar nahin aati  
> Koi soorat nazar nahin aati[^1]

**English translation:**  
> No hope I have is ever answered,  
> No way ahead can be uncovered.[^2]

This refrain of despair ("nahin aati" — "does not come") echoes through the ghazal's 10 couplets, but the first is iconic for directly evoking hopelessness in love, life, and fate — a hallmark of Ghalib's style in *Diwan-e-Ghalib*.[^3]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/115-koi-ummeed-bar-nahin-aati/transliteration/#p-6396af).
[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/115-koi-ummeed-bar-nahin-aati/translation/#p-92c600).
[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/115-koi-ummeed-bar-nahin-aati/translation/).

cases.prompt: In Iqbal's 'Shikwa', show me the verse where the speaker complains directly to God about the fate of Muslims.
cases.rationale: Tests retrieval inside Bang-e-Dara Part 3 chapter 20, the famous complaint poem.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Allama Iqbal's *Shikwa* (from *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*), the speaker—representing the Muslim ummah—directly addresses God (referred to as "O God" or implied through context) with a lament about their past glories contrasted against their current degradation, disgrace, and loss of worldly favor to non-Muslims/infidel foes.[^1]

One key opening verse establishing the direct complaint:

> Forgive my tongue, but God, I have a grievance to declare.[^2]

This builds into explicit grievances about Muslims' fate, such as:

> But all Your blessings fall upon the houses of the foe,  
> While on the wretched Muslims falls the lightning’s fiery blow.[^3]

> The idols in the temples say, “The Muslims now are gone!”  
> They celebrate that guardians of the Kaaba have withdrawn.  
> ...  
> The unbelievers mock us now—do You feel this or not?  
> The Creed of Your own Oneness—have You utterly forgot?[^4]

> The outrage is that infidels get nymphs and mansions there,  
> While for the wretched Muslim, just the promise of a prayer.  
> ...  
> Why is the wealth of this world now so scarce for Muslim hands?  
> ...  
> We face the taunts of strangers, disgrace, and poverty;  
> Is this the prize for those who gave their very lives for Thee?[^5]

> The world has now become the friend of those who are not ours,  
> And for ourselves, a world of dreams is all that now empowers.  
> We have departed from the stage; others possess its towers.[^6]

The full poem elaborates this theme throughout, juxtaposing Muslim heroism (e.g., conquering for God's name) with their present subjugation.

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a saint who addresses a marble pillar inside a heathen city, calling it 'better than gold' because the King of glory once inscribed the Ten Commandments on it and gave it to Moses (and 'as the truth-fast ones afterwards held it… Joshua and Tobias'), then commands it to spew a 'wide-running water for the ruin of men' upon the corrupt folk? Quote the saint's address to the stone and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The fusion of an animate marble pillar (figured as the original Sinai tablet kept by Joshua and Tobias) with a flood unleashed inside a city to drown its inhabitants is a signature, narratively unique passage of this work; no other Old English Christian poem in the corpus features the pillar-flood image, and the dharmaśāstra/Old Javanese/19th-century works are unrelated genres.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Andreas* by Cynewulf (an anonymous Old English poem attributed to him), Chapter 1.**[^1]

Saint Andreas (Andrew) addresses a marble pillar ("marble-stone") beside a hall wall in the heathen city of Mermedonia. He calls it "better than gold" because the King of Glory inscribed the Ten Commandments ("the righteous law / He marked out in ten words") on it and gave it to Moses, and "as the truth-fast ones afterwards held it… Joshua and Tobias." He then commands it to release "wide-running water for the ruin of men."[^2]

> “Hear me now, marble-stone, by the Maker's decree,  
> before whose face all of creation  
> becomes afraid, when they see the Father  
> of heavens and earth with the greatest of hosts  
> on middle-earth seeking mankind.  
> Let now from your foundations streams start to well,  
> a river in-flooding, for the Almighty now,  
> Heaven's High King, commands you to hasten  
> and upon this corrupt folk to send forth  
> a wide-running water for the ruin of men,  
> a pouring ocean. Behold, you are better than gold,  
> than treasure-gifts! On you the King Himself  
> wrote, the God of glory, He made known in words  
> the ready mysteries, and the righteous law  
> He marked out in ten words,  
> the Maker mighty in power. To Moses He gave it,  
> as the truth-fast ones afterwards held it,  
> the brave young thanes, his own kinsmen,  
> the God-fearing men, Joshua and Tobias.  
> Now you can know that the King of angels  
> adorned you far more with gifts in old days  
> than all the kindred of gems.  
> Through His holy command you must quickly show  
> if you have any understanding of Him.”[^2]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [Chapter 1](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/).

[^2]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-2e50c1).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus stages a dialogue at sea in which a saint, not yet recognizing that his helmsman is the disguised Lord of glory, praises him as a wave-rider who is 'young, not old in winters' yet steers a 'foam-necked' ship that fares 'most like a fowl' — and then says he himself has been 'sixteen times on a sea-boat' yet has never seen such a steersman? Quote the saint's praise of the helmsman and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: A specific numerical boast (sixteen prior sea-voyages) embedded in a Christ-as-disguised-helmsman dialogue is unique to this work. The Seafarer in the Old English Elegies has a sea-voyager but no disguised-Christ dialogue, no foam-necked-fowl simile, and no sixteen-voyages count; Elene and Juliana have no sea-journey at all.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Andreas* by Cynewulf (an anonymous Anglo-Saxon poet who signed his name in runes), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Old English poem stages the described dialogue at sea between Saint Andrew (Andreas) and the disguised Christ, who serves as the helmsman of their miraculously calm ship en route to rescue Matthew from the Mermedonians. Andrew praises the helmsman (still unrecognized as the Lord of Glory) for his extraordinary skill, noting his youth ("young, shelter of warriors, not old in winters"), calling him a "wave-rider," describing the ship as "foam-necked" and faring "most like a fowl," and confessing he has been "'sixteen times on a sea-boat' yet [has] never seen such a steersman."

Here is Andrew's full praise (the concluding speech of his dialogue with the helmsman):

> “Never have I met a better seaman,
> more skilled in his craft, so it seems to me,
> a stronger rower, a shrewder guide,
> wiser in his words. I wish to ask you,
> uncorrupted hero, for one more boon,
> though I have few rings, or rich treasures,
> or hammered gold to hand you now.
> I would win your friendship, glorious chieftain,
> your good will, if I could but gain it.
> For this you will reap a reward,
> a holy hope in heaven’s high glory,
> if you will grant your grace and your lore
> to us, the sea-weary. I wish to seek from you,
> battle-brave hero, one certain skill:
> that you would teach me, since the true King
> and Shaper of men gave you such strength,
> how you hold the wave-floater steady,
> the sea-stallion, and steer it on the sound.
> I have been, in my time, now and before,
> **sixteen times on a sea-boat**,
> my hands frozen while stirring the mere,
> the ocean-streams—this is one time more—
> yet I have never seen any man,
> any son of heroes, who is like to you,
> steering at the stern. The stream’s-welling stills,
> the surf beats the shore. This boat is so swift,
> **it fares, foam-necked, most like a fowl**,
> gliding on the ocean. I know for a fact
> that I have never seen on the wave-path
> in any seafarer such a strange skill.
> It is most like as if it stood still
> on the shore of the land, where no storm can stir it,
> no wind can awaken it, no water-floods
> break its broad prow, yet on the brine it speeds,
> swift under sail. **You yourself are young**,
> **shelter of warriors, not old in winters**,
> **yet you have in your heart, you wave-rider**,
> the answer of a hero. You know for every word
> in the world its wise understanding.”[^2]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [Chapter 1](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/).
[^2]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ca9772).

Elene's True-Cross narrative, Juliana's martyrdom, and the anonymous Old English elegies contain no such jailbreak or count; the cannibal-city setting and Mermedonian frame are exclusive to Andreas.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Andreas* by Cynewulf (work slug: `cynewulf-andreas-07b573`), an Old English poem narrating the apostle Andrew's (Andreas) mission to rescue Matthew from the cannibalistic city of Mermedonia.**[^1]

This occurs in **Chapter 1**, where Andrew approaches the prison: seven guards ("before the barred doors guards were standing, seven together") drop dead at his approach ("Sudden death took them all, they fell, fame-less"). The door opens by divine power, and inside he embraces his fellow apostle Matthew ("Each one embraced the other with his arms, they kissed and they clasped"). Andrew then leads out the captives: **"one hundred and two, counted in number, and also forty, saved from the strife— he left not one of them under the city-lock fastened in fetters—and of women there yet, in addition to the host, one less than fifty he freed, the fearful."**[^2]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus rules that if a Brahmin's top-knot is cut by enemies five times, his Brahminhood is irrecoverably lost — explicitly stating that even a fresh saṃskāra cannot restore it — while in earlier passages it had laid out that for a single cutting one wears hair from a cow's tail on the ear and performs a Prājāpatya penance, and for cutting in the middle one is again purified by a Prājāpatya? Quote the verses on the fivefold cutting and the cow's-tail remedy verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The exact threshold of five enemy-cuttings as the irreversible loss of Brahminhood, combined with the specific intermediate remedies (cow's-tail hair on the ear plus Prājāpatya for a single cutting; Prājāpatya for a midway cutting), is unique to this short prayaścitta smṛti; Manu, Yājñavalkya, Parāśara, Yama, Viṣṇu and the other corpus smṛtis do not enumerate this graded top-knot rule with the five-strikes-and-out clause.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Chapter 1.**

Earlier in the chapter, for a **single cutting** of a Brahmin's top-knot (*śikhā*) by an enemy, the remedy is: "If his top-knot is cut by an enemy, he should immediately wear hair from a cow's tail on his ear and, being pure, should perform a Prājāpatya penance."[^1] "Through a new *saṃskāra*, he becomes pure; there is no doubt in this."[^2]

For **cutting in the middle**: "If the top-knot is cut in the middle, he is purified by a Prājāpatya penance."[^3]

**For five cuttings** by enemies: "If the top-knot is cut by enemies five times, his Brahminhood is lost, even with a new *saṃskāra*."[^4]

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does a Sanskrit penitential text declare that one who eats in the same row, or together with, a woman who has been married multiple times must be buried covered in a pit dug in the earth for either one watch (three hours) or two ghaṭikās (forty-eight minutes), then perform the birth-rites and other saṃskāras anew, perform a thousand Taptakṛcchra penances, subsist on barley while performing the Cāpāgrayāna rite, and bathe five thousand times — either himself or through a Brahmin — to be purified for himself alone, the rule attributed to Bhṛgu in ancient times? Quote the passage verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific compound penance — burial in an earthen pit for the precise alternative durations of one watch / two ghaṭikās, followed by exactly a thousand Taptakṛcchra penances and five thousand baths (with the option of a Brahmin proxy), attributed to Bhṛgu, for the offense of merely eating in the same row with a multiply-married woman — is uniquely this smṛti's formulation; the other dharmaśāstras and digests in the corpus (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Parāśara, Yama, Viṣṇu, Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana, Vīramitrodaya) do not pair these specific numbers with this specific transgression.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**In the Āṅgirasa Smṛti (Chapter 1), verses 219–223.**

A woman who has been married one, two, three, four, or five times, in very vile times that are full of sin—if she is discovered, one should question her well, investigate, and deliberate.[^1] [...] A *veśyā* (prostitute), though fit only for pleasure, is superior to her. If one eats in the same row with her during these rites, or eats together with her, it is a wicked act that causes loss of caste.[^2] For the purification of that sin, one should, according to rule, be covered in a pit in the earth, having dug it for a period of one watch (three hours) or two *ghaṭikās* (forty-eight minutes). After being lifted out of it, one should then perform the birth rites (*jātakarma*) and others anew. One should also perform a thousand Taptakṛcchra penances according to rule. With a controlled self, subsisting on barley, he should perform the Cāpāgrayāna rite; then he becomes pure. He should perform five thousand baths, either himself or through a Brahmin. Then he becomes pure for himself alone. He is not fit for others. Thus spoke Bhṛgu in ancient times.[^3]

cases.prompt: Which Sanskrit dharmaśāstra, in its enumeration of the twelve kinds of witnesses, describes a category in which a man is hidden behind a wall and made to overhear a statement about a debt, so that — when the debt is later denied — he can come forward and report what he heard? Quote the verse that defines this concealed witness, and name the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The 'concealed by a wall, made to hear a statement about a debt' formulation of the gūḍha-sākṣin appears verbatim in Bṛhaspati's twelve-witness scheme; Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Kātyāyana etc. either lack this category entirely or do not phrase it with the wall-eavesdropper image.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Bṛhaspati Smṛti (Chapter 1, section 1.5 on Witnesses)** enumerates twelve kinds of witnesses: a subscribed witness, a recorded witness, a *concealed witness* (gūḍha), a reminded witness, a kinsman, a messenger, a chance witness, a secondary witness, one present at the transaction, a king, a chief judge, and the village.[^1]

The concealed witness (*gūḍha sākṣin*) is defined as follows: "He who, concealed by a wall, is made to hear a statement about a debt, and who, when it is denied, relates it as it happened, is called a concealed witness." // Brh_1,5.8 //[^2]

cases.prompt: Which Sanskrit legal treatise warns against forged copies of written instruments by comparing them to a reflection in a mirror — saying that just as a reflection appears real though it is unreal, so skilled persons make copies of documents? Quote the simile and identify the work and chapter section on documents.
cases.rationale: The mirror-reflection simile for document forgery (Brh_1,6.33) is a distinctive Bṛhaspati image embedded in his elaborate treatment of lekhya, which goes well beyond the document-rules of Manu, Yājñavalkya or Nārada; no other smṛti in the corpus uses this metaphor.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The Bṛhaspati Smṛti (Bṛhaspati Smṛti), an ancient Sanskrit legal treatise attributed to the sage Bṛhaspati, contains this exact warning in its sole chapter (Chapter 1), specifically in the section on documents (marked as Brh_1,6.33).**[^1]

The passage cautions that a document with indistinct letters becomes equivalent to a forgery, then employs the requested simile: **"Just as a reflection in a mirror, though unreal, appears as real, so skilled persons make copies of documents."** This immediately follows a note on how poor writing leads to forgery (Brh_1,6.32).[^2]

**This passage appears in Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété*, Volume I, Chapter 4 ("On the occupation of things").**[^1]

He argues this "universal right" is a "chimera," as it would absurdly require global consent for basic acts of survival:

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does an author ridicule the doctrine that every invention is the natural property of its inventor by tracing it to absurd conclusions — that the first man to turn wood into clogs or animal skin into sandals would acquire 'the exclusive right to shoe the human race', so that an inhabitant of the austral pole could not wear clogs without buying them from an inventor at the boreal pole, and that the first cultivator of wheat would have left the rest of mankind reduced to eating snakes, rats, or frogs, or even one another, to remain faithful to the rights of nature? cases.rationale: The clogs/sandals reductio with the boreal-pole/austral-pole geography and the snakes-rats-frogs trio is a distinctive verbatim image attacking the 1790 Constituent Assembly's patent-as-natural-right doctrine; it is specific to this work's treatment of industrial-invention property.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte ridicules the doctrine that every invention is the natural property of its inventor in *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, Chapter 29 ("De la propriété des inventions ou des procédés industriels.").**[^1][^2]

He traces it to absurd conclusions through reductio ad absurdum, first with footwear:

> The first man who conceived and executed the idea of transforming a piece of wood into a pair of clogs, or an animal skin into a pair of sandals, acquired by this sole fact the exclusive right to shoe the human race. From that moment, all men found themselves under the obligation to walk barefoot, or to go and provide themselves with footwear from the fortunate inventor. If the discovery was made by an inhabitant of the boreal pole, the inhabitants of the austral pole could not, without injuring the rights of man and without violating the principles engraved in all hearts, permit themselves to wear clogs without having bought them from the other extremity of the globe.[^1]

Then with agriculture:

From that moment, the peoples of all races, white, black, yellow, red, and swarthy, had to cross the seas and the mountains to go and provide themselves with bread from the inventor. Those who could not make the journey were obliged to continue eating snakes, rats, or frogs, or even to eat one another, to remain faithful to the rights of nature.[^2]

**In Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Chapter 13 ("Distinction entre un régime arbitraire et un régime légal. De ce qui constitue la différence"), this argument appears prominently.[^1][^2]**

Comte contends that the distinction between "arbitrary" and "legal" rule is often overstated or illusory, as a malevolent power can be rendered perfectly regular and codified without becoming benevolent. > PER ME SI VA NELL’ ETERNO DOLORE :  
> PER ME SI VA TRA LA PERDUTA GENTE.  
> GIUSTIZIA MOSSE ‘L MIO ALTO FATTORE.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation*, Volume I (Chapter 21),** argues precisely this.[^1]

Comte contends that vices like prostitution produce pleasures for the individual but also natural pains (misery, contempt, disease, etc.) that repress them. Charitable institutions — foundling hospitals (free delivery for unwed mothers), orphanages for illegitimate children, disease hospitals for the afflicted, and asylums for "repentant" prostitutes — weaken these repressive pains for the vicious while leaving the pleasures intact, thus encouraging vice.[^2] He illustrates with the career of prostitution, showing how each institution successively eases its burdens (maternity, child-rearing, illness, social exclusion), making "vice... multiply to infinity."[^3]

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
In Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, Chapter 6 ("Des difficultés que présente la question de l’influence des climats sur les facultés humaines. — Exposition of Montesquieu's system on this influence. — Vices of this system"), Comte discredits Montesquieu's climate theory (derived in part from Chardin) by mocking its basis in a bizarre physiological experiment on "half a sheep’s tongue."[^1][^2]

The key passage describing the experiment is:

> In adopting this system, Montesquieu nevertheless did not wish to rely blindly on Chardin’s opinion; he subjected this opinion to the test of facts, and the experiment by which he judged it is of such an extraordinary nature that it would be unbelievable if he had not himself recorded it in *The Spirit of the Laws.* The philosopher-jurist took half a sheep’s tongue; he subjected it alternately to a hot temperature, and to a cold temperature down to freezing; he examined, with the aid of a magnifying glass, the effects produced by the cold and by the heat on this half-tongue, and these effects served him to determine the influence that cold and heat exercise on the physical and moral nature of man, in all parts of our globe. Here are the results of this singular experiment.[^1]

Comte then summarizes Montesquieu's sweeping conclusions from this (e.g., northerners are brave and frank but dull and drunken; southerners are timid, lazy, sensual, and suited to despotism), before dismissing the whole theory:

> Although little versed in physiology, I have difficulty persuading myself that these facts can be ascertained by experiments done on half a sheep’s tongue; and even if they were ascertained by such experiments, I cannot see any link between them and the moral consequences that Montesquieu deduces from them. **I confess that a moral and political system that rested on such experiments would seem to me to rest on a scarcely solid base.**[^2]

cases.rationale: The exact numerical progression 50/51/58/69 attached to Diemen's Land, New Holland, Timor, and the French (drawn from Péron's voyage to the southern lands) is a unique, verbatim-quotable factoid present only in this work; it could not be retrieved from the Comte volumes or any other corpus item.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The dynamometer is cited as evidence against Rousseau's claim in **Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Chapter 5 ("Liberté compatible avec la manière de vivre des peuples sauvages")**.[^1]

The key passage giving the progression reads:

> He remarked that physical vigor, on this scale of civilization, followed the **following progression: 50, 51, 58, 69** [^93]; **that is to say, that the savages of Diemen's Land had been able, on average, to make the pressure needle of the dynamometer move only up to 50 degrees, those of New Holland to 51, and those of Timor to 58; whereas the French, despite the weakening resulting for them from the fatigues of a very long and very arduous navigation, had made it advance to 69** [^94].[^1]

This follows Dunoyer's discussion of Péron's experiments (contrasting Europeans and various natives) and immediately precedes his concession that racial differences might play some role, while reaffirming that "civilization is favorable to the development and extension of physical forces." Rousseau's claim (that the savage is physically stronger) is quoted and critiqued earlier in the same chapter.[^2]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [p-910272](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/05-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-pe/translation/#p-910272).
[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [p-646b23](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/05-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-pe/translation/#p-646b23).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus reconstructs the etymology of the word 'industry' from the Latin INDU (for INTUS, 'within') joined to STRUO ('I arrange, I set up, I construct'), and uses this philology to argue that the term was first applied to the cunning of swindlers — so that 'knights of industry' originally named men distinguished in fraud — citing the Abbé Galiani's claim, 'formally' written in 1750, that no fortune could grow without other fortunes being diminished? Quote the passage that gives the INTUS-STRUO derivation and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The combination of the INDU/INTUS-STRUO etymology, the 'knights of industry' (chevaliers d'industrie) gloss, and the dated 1750 reference to Abbé Galiani is a distinctive philological-economic argument unique to this work; no other corpus item (including the Comte legislation/property volumes) reconstructs the word 'industry' in this way.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Chapter 11 ("I. Liberté compatible avec la manière de vivre des peuples purement industrieux").**[^1]

The requested etymology appears in the opening of §1:

> § 1. The word *industry* is a nearly literal translation of the Latin word INDUSTRIA. Etymologists suppose that the latter must have been formed from two others, INDU for INTUS, *within*, and STRUO, *I arrange, I set up, I construct*. Thus one would have said INDUSTRIA from INTUS-STRUO, INTUS-STRUERE, *to construct within*, an action exercised in things or on things to arrive at a certain end.[^1]

Dunoyer uses this philology to explain the word's early application to cunning or fraud (e.g., "knights of industry" as "men distinguished in the art of fraud"), linking it to pre-modern views of wealth as zero-sum. He cites the Abbé Galiani's 1750 claim in the same chapter:

> The Abbé Galiani, who was one of the wittiest writers of his time, and one of the most enlightened on matters of public economy, wrote formally, in 1750, that a fortune could not grow without other fortunes being diminished.[^2]

Quote the relevant passages and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The three-name sequence of Arkwright-as-barber, Newcomen-as-hardware-dealer/Cawley-as-blacksmith, and Watt-at-Glasgow-1764-as-craftsman, deployed together to argue that art preceded science in industry, is unique to this work. No other corpus item (the Comte legislation/property volumes, Dunoyer Vol I, Fichte, Iqbal, Ghalib, Zauq, Cynewulf, the Sanskrit dharmasastras, or the Old Javanese texts) discusses these specific industrial inventors or this empirical-vs-theoretical thesis.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Chapter 16 ("VI. De la liberté de l'industrie manufacturière" / "VI. On the liberty of the manufacturing industry").**[^1]

Dunoyer argues against the view that industrial progress derives primarily from scientific theory, emphasizing instead empirical methods by "groping" inventors. He stacks three biographical examples in succession:

> Arkwright, the author of the spinning machine, one of the most ingenious and powerful to have been invented in fifty years, **the barber Arkwright had no scientific instruction, and judged only by groping, by trying, by experimenting,** on the effect that each of the pieces of his admirable machine would produce.[^2]

> **Newcomen and Cawley, two mechanics who contributed appreciably to the progress of the steam engine, and the first who sought and succeeded in drawing useful effects from it, were, one a hardware dealer, the other a blacksmith,** and neither could pass for a scholar.[^3]

> **Watt himself, the illustrious Watt, when he began to make that series of perfections to the steam engine which have made it so powerful a motor, must not have possessed much scientific knowledge**... **when, in 1764, [he] was charged by the University of Glasgow... with repairing a Newcomen machine, it was simply in the capacity of a craftsman,** [having] been able to study only until the age of sixteen in one of those elementary and free schools that exist in Scotland under the name of *Grammar school.*[^4]

These examples illustrate his broader claim: "the two most admirable instruments, perhaps, that human industry possesses, the spinning machine and the fire-engine, could be, the first invented by a man absolutely deprived of scientific instruction, and the second much perfected by men little or not at all versed in theory."[^5]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [Chapter 16](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/).

cases.prompt: Where in the corpus does the author illustrate his warning that 'overly adventurous speculators' do more harm to industry than mere dissipators by recounting that 'a great Russian lord, known for the liberality of his sentiments, asked one of our chemists to teach him how to distill potatoes' — adding that this lord ardently wished to excite his peasants by example, and declared 'that he wished to make no profit from it, and that he would even consent to lose a great deal on it' — at which the chemist replied 'God forbid that I should teach you an art in which you would consent to suffer losses'? cases.rationale: The Russian-lord/potato-distillation anecdote with the chemist's 'God forbid' rebuke is a vivid, verbatim-quotable parable unique to this work. None of the other corpus titles — including the sister Comte volumes on legislation and property, Dunoyer Vol I (which covers theory rather than concrete manufacturing examples), Fichte, the poetry collections, or the Sanskrit/Old Javanese ritual-and-law texts — contains this story or anything resembling it.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The anecdote appears in Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II* (English translation), Chapter 16 ("VI. De la liberté de l'industrie manufacturière").**[^1]

Dunoyer illustrates the warning there as follows:

> If the lack of zeal, application, and care deprives manufacturing of much strength, the *lack of prudence* does it scarcely less harm. A manufacturer who ruins himself does not cause it damage only because he destroys a part of its means, but also because he is a bad example. Dissipators are perhaps less harmful to it than overly adventurous speculators. Both attack its resources; but the latter have the particular fault of discrediting it. Only a small number of imprudent and unfortunate trials are needed to decry a new industry which, wisely practiced, could have become very productive, and usefully attracted many hands and much capital to it. Thus the greatest enemies of the useful arts are not so much perhaps those who spend their fortune on festivities, on pleasures, as those who dissipate it in ill-conceived and ill-conceived enterprises, where, while pretending to encourage industry, they teach the industrious only the secret of ruining themselves. A great Russian lord, known for the liberality of his sentiments, asked one of our chemists to teach him how to distill potatoes; he ardently desired, he said, to excite by his example the inhabitants of his lands to devote themselves to this kind of fabrication: for the rest, he declared *that he wished to make no profit from it, and that he would even consent to lose a great deal on it*.  
> 
> “God forbid,” replied the learned man, “that I should teach you an art in which you would consent to suffer losses; it is a poor service to industry to make such a use of one's means; the only enterprises whose example is favorable to it are those that are lucrative, and to form such ones, one must bring to them an attention and a prudence that one hardly takes the trouble to apply when one has neither the need, nor the desire to obtain profits.”[^1]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [paragraphs](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-06b6eb).

**Cynewulf, *Elene*, Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Old English verse poem narrates the Finding of the True Cross by Helena (Elene), mother of Constantine. The queen is Elene; the reluctant Hebrew informant is Judas, whom her men thrust "into a dry well, where, deprived of all comforts, he sat in his sorrows for a space of seven nights under a harmful lock, hunger-haunted, clasped by fetters"; they then lead him to Calvary hill.

**Depth-and-three-crosses passage:**  
"he began to dig for the tree of glory,  
resolute, delving the earth under the turf,  
so that at twenty feet deep he found,  
hidden below, concealed under the ness  
in a dark cavern, three crosses together  
in that dreary hall, buried in grit"[^2]

**Corpse-revival passage:**  
"Then a multitude came,  
no small folk, and a dead man they brought  
on a bier in a throng of men nearby  
(it was then the ninth hour), a youth, spiritless.  
Then Judas was in his mind-spirit  
greatly gladdened. He commanded them to set down  
the soulless one, the life-departed body  
on the earth, the unliving, and he, a champion of right,  
raised up two of the roods in his arms,  
the deep-thinking, over that fated house.  
It was dead as before, the corpse fast in its lair.  
The limbs grew cold, covered in death-pangs.  
Then the third was raised, the holy one.  
The body was in waiting until upon it  
the prince’s Rood was raised,  
the Sky-King’s beam, the true victory-beacon.  
He at once arose, with spirit endowed,  
together both body and soul."[^3]

No other corpus work (Comte, Dunoyer, the Sanskrit smṛtis, Old English poems, Iqbal, Ghalib) combines an arithmetical Trinity gibe with a critique of authoritarian truth-determination.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Johann Gottlieb Fichte**, in *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens* (A Demand for Freedom of Thought from the Princes of Europe), **Chapter 2**, satirizes princely truth-by-fiat in this way:

> Therefore, imitate your worthy models entirely, you princes; reject today what you commanded to be believed yesterday, and authorize today what you rejected yesterday, so that they never become unaccustomed to the idea that your will alone is the source of truth. You have, for example, for too long willed that one equals three; they have believed you, and unfortunately, they have become so accustomed to it that they have long since withheld the gratitude due to you, and believe they discovered it themselves. Avenge your authority; command, just once, that one equals one—not, of course, because the opposite contradicts itself, but because you will it.[^1]

This occurs in the context of Fichte mocking rulers' claims to define truth arbitrarily, contrasting it with the wisdom of early Christian emperors who frequently altered doctrine with each reign to keep subjects reliant on authority alone.[^2]

**The work is *Juliana* by Cynewulf, Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Old English poem recounts the martyrdom of Saint Juliana of Nicomedia. The persecutor (Eleusius, prompted by a devil) orders an earthen (clay) vessel filled with lead and set atop a massive pyre—"a bath boiled hot"—into which the saint is thrown. The lead bursts open, killing exactly seventy-five heathens, while Juliana emerges unscathed: "Not her veil nor her robe, / not her hair nor her skin was marked by the fire, / not her body nor limbs."

> The fiend was not slow, who taught him to have  
> an earthen vessel made with wondrous craft,  
> and piled high with wood. Then the hard man commanded  
> that the clay vessel be filled with lead,  
> and ordered the greatest of pyres to be kindled,  
> a bale-fire lit, surrounded on all sides  
> with brands. The bath boiled hot. Then, swollen with anger,  
> he ordered the faultless one to be shoved,  
> guiltless, into the surging lead. The flame was split apart,  
> the fire loosed. The lead sprang wide,  
> hot and sword-greedy. The men were terrified,  
> startled by the rush. There, in number, burned  
> by the fire’s blast, **seventy-five**  
> of the **heathen host**. Yet the holy one stood  
> with her beauty unblemished. **Not her veil nor her robe**,  
> **not her hair nor her skin** was marked by the fire,  
> **not her body nor limbs**. She stood in the flame  
> entirely sound, and gave thanks for it all  
> to the Lord of lords.[^1]

This chapter enumerates woman's separate property (*strīdhana*) as six-fold in verse K_894:

> What is given before the nuptial fire (*adhyagni*), what is given at the bridal procession (*adhyāvāhanika*), and what is given to a woman out of affection, and what is received from a brother, mother, or father—Woman's Property (*strīdhana*) is remembered as six-fold.[^1]

Within this scheme, the category *pādavandanika*—a gift made for bowing at the feet of one's mother-in-law or father-in-law—is grouped together with what is given out of affection (*prītidatta*) and defined in verse K_897:

> Whatever is given out of affection by a mother-in-law or a father-in-law, and what is given for bowing at their feet (*pādavandanika*), is called a gift of affection (*prītidatta*).

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus in which an ascetic on a hell-tour notices a copper cauldron being scrubbed clean in the courtyard of the Lord of Death and asks why an empty vessel is being washed. The Lord of Death answers that a great sinner is expected within seven nights, that his term in that cauldron will run for a hundred thousand years, and that afterwards he will be carried by weapon-birds to be impaled on an iron tree and then hung head-down from a bamboo pole over a fire on the iron plain. Quote the lines in which the visitor asks about the empty cauldron, and the lines in which the Lord of Death gives the seven-nights-and-hundred-thousand-years answer. Identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The cauldron-being-washed-in-advance scene with the precise schedule (seven-night wait, hundred-thousand-year sentence, weapon-birds, iron-tree, bamboo pole over fire) is uniquely the hell-tour of the Old Javanese narrative on Kuñjarakarṇa. The other Old Javanese Buddhist works in the corpus (San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna, Kalpabuddha) are doctrinal tattva texts with no hell-tour narrative; the Sanskrit smṛtis describe hells in lists, not as a personal walking tour with a cauldron being prepared in advance for a named arriving soul.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana** (anonymous), *Chapter 1* (the only chapter in this work).[^1]

The ascetic Kuñjarakarṇa, sent by the Buddha on a tour of hell, arrives at Yama's palace and sees a "great copper cauldron with many spouts... in its courtyard."[^2] He asks Yama about it being scrubbed clean while empty:

> \"Lord Yama, may your glory be eternal.  
> Your compassion for this Kuñjara is fruitful.  
> But what is that cauldron that is being washed?  
> What is its purpose, now that it is clean?\"[^3]  
>   
> \"I am puzzled by it, for it is to be filled,  
> yet it is turned upside down, with nothing inside.  
> What is the reason for it being so empty?  
> This is my question; please tell me.\"[^4]

Yama replies that a great sinner (the gandharva king Pūrṇavijaya) is due soon, with his term in the cauldron lasting a hundred thousand years:

> \"There is a great sinner who is to be cast in.  
> His time in torment will be long.  
> For a hundred thousand years is his term;  
> when it is full, he will be in the cauldron of misery.\"[^5]  
>   
> \"But as for his arrival in Yama's realm,  
> in seven nights he is expected to come.  
> That is the reason the cauldron is being washed,  
> as the place of his torment when he arrives.\"[^6]

Yama adds that afterward, weapon-birds will carry the sinner to an iron tree (to be dashed on its sword-blades), then he will be hung head-down from a bamboo pole over ceaseless fire on the iron plain.[^7]

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus that describes a hell-torment reversed by meditation: on the tenth night a sinner holds a pure mind in the cauldron, the cauldron shatters and its fire dies, becoming a jeweled lotus pond, while the nearby sword-tree is transmuted into a Pārijāta whose fruits are flawless Kaustubha jewels and whose flowers are golden necklaces, its blood-sap turned exceedingly sweet-smelling. Quote the verses in which the cauldron becomes the jeweled lotus pond and in which the sword-tree becomes the Pārijāta with Kaustubha-jewel fruits. Identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific transformation — cauldron into jeweled lotus pond, sword-tree into a Pārijāta whose fruits are named as Kaustubha jewels, all triggered by the sinner's tenth-night meditation — is a unique narrative climax in the Kuñjarakarṇa text. The doctrinal Old Javanese Buddhist tattvas (San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan, Mahājñāna, Tattvajñāna, Kalpabuddha) do not narrate a hell-becoming-paradise scene; the Saiva tattvas (Gaṇapati-, Vṛhaspati-tattva) and Old Javanese codes (Slokantara, Vratisasana) likewise lack any such transmutation episode.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana**, Chapter 1.[^1]

The cauldron of hell is transmuted into a jeweled lotus pond when the sinner maintains a pure mind through meditation on the tenth night:

> Now, he who was in hell, it is told, on his ninth night,  
> There he became mindful, and the sharpness of the cauldron felt as nothing.  
> When the tenth night arrived, he held to a meditation of pure mind.  
> The cauldron was shattered, its fire died, transformed into a jeweled lotus pond.[^2]

The nearby sword-tree (weapon-tree) is transmuted into a glorious Pārijāta tree:

> And the sword-tree became a lovely Pārijāta, all glorious.  
> Its fruits were flawless gems, Kaustubha jewels, its flowers golden necklaces.  
> Its soft leaves were a fragrant footstool, its blood-sap exceedingly sweet-smelling.  
> It ceased to be foul, sharp-smelling, and pungent; all its qualities were reversed, bringing bliss.[^3]

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus in which a Buddha-figure, instructing a pupil on the origin of the body, says that the seed emerged from the father's liṅga 'like molten tin, poured out,' that Kāma is its name at that moment while Ratih is the name when it clung to the mother, and that the embryo then took form in 'the blossoming lotus of your mother's womb' — paired with a five-souls scheme (ātmā, parātmā, antarātmā, nirātmā, cetanātmā) joined to the five great elements. Quote the verse with the molten-tin / Kāma-and-Ratih image, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific embryological metaphor — Kāma as the father's seed 'like molten tin' from the liṅga, Ratih as the same drop after clinging to the mother — set inside a Buddhist sermon and paired with the five-souls + five-elements scheme, is uniquely Kuñjarakarṇa's instruction scene. The other Old Javanese Buddhist tattvas discuss the five souls doctrinally but do not place them inside a Kāma/Ratih-from-the-liṅga embryology; the Saiva tattvas also lack this exact paired image, as do the Sanskrit smṛtis and the Old English / Urdu / French works.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The passage is from the *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana* (unknown author), Chapter 1.**[^1]

In this Old Javanese Buddhist didactic poem, the Buddha (appearing as Vairocana and also identified with Śākyamuni) instructs the pupil(s) Kuñjarakarṇa (an ascetic *rākṣasa*) and Pūrṇavijaya (a *gandharva*/*vidyādhara* king) on the origins of the body during a sermon on karma, rebirth, and the soul. The Buddha explains the seed's emission and embryonic formation precisely as described.[^2]

The relevant verse (Canto 17.8) reads:

> It was from the passion of your father and mother that they were moved to unite.  
> From the *liṅga* of your father you emerged, like molten tin, poured out.  
> Indeed, Kāma is its name; Ratih is your name when you clung to your mother.  
> There in the blossoming lotus of your mother's womb, you took on a definite form.[^3]

This is immediately followed by the embryo's association with the five great elements (earth, water, fire, wind, ether), each linked to one of the five souls (*ātmā*, *parātmā*, *antarātmā*, *nirātmā*, *cetanātmā*), forming the body (Cantos 18–19). The scheme is first introduced earlier by Yama (Canto 11.17), but the Buddha elaborates it here in the context of bodily origin.[^4]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus opens with a cosmogony in which a divine being splits a golden egg in two to form heaven and earth, then produces the four social orders from his mouth, arms, thighs, and feet? Quote the relevant verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The combined hiranyagarbha (golden-egg) cosmogony followed immediately by the mouth/arms/thighs/feet origin of the varnas in a single opening chapter is distinctive to this work.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Manusmṛti* (also known as the *Laws of Manu*), Chapter 1.**

This chapter opens with a cosmogony matching your description precisely. The divine Self-Existent One (Svayambhu) creates waters, deposits his seed therein, which becomes a golden egg. He is reborn within it as Brahma, dwells a year, then splits it to form heaven and earth (among other things). Later in the same creation sequence, he produces the four *varnas* (social orders)—Brahmana, Kshatriya, Vaishya, and Shudra—from his mouth, arms, thighs, and feet.[^1][^2][^3][^4]

Here are the verbatim verses (in translation):

> That seed became a **golden egg**, in splendor like the thousand-rayed sun; in it he himself was born as Brahma, the great father of all the worlds. // Mn_1.9 //[^1]
>
> After dwelling in that **egg** for a full year, that divine Lord, by his own contemplation, **split the egg in two**. // Mn_1.12 //[^2]
>
> And with those two halves he formed the **heavens and the earth**, and between them the middle region, the eight directions, and the eternal abode of the waters. // Mn_1.13 //[^3]
>
> And for the sake of the prosperity of the worlds, he caused the **Brahmana, the Kshatriya, the Vaishya, and the Shudra** to emanate from his **mouth, arms, thighs, and feet**. // Mn_1.31 //[^4]

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1.**

This text pairs a doctrine of Dharma's progressive decline across the four yugas—with all dharmas arising fully in the Kṛta and being lost by the Kali—with age-specific supreme or "highest" dharmas: austerity (*tapas*) in the Kṛta, knowledge (*jñāna*) in the Tretā, sacrifice (*yajña*) in the Dvāpara, and giving (*dāna*) alone in the Kali.[^1][^2][^3]

The key verses are Par_1.16 (noting Dharma's full emergence and eventual loss), Par_1.22 (dharmas vary by yuga), and especially Par_1.23 (explicitly naming the highest dharma per age). Par_1.24 adds that Parāśara's own teachings apply to the Kali.[^4]

[^1]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-d9b4b0](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-d9b4b0). "In this Manvantara, there are *dharmas* for the Kṛta, Tretā, and other *yugas*. All *dharmas* arose in the Kṛta *yuga*; all are lost in the Kali *yuga*."

[^2]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-11656a](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-11656a). "The *dharmas* for men are different in the Kṛta *yuga*, the Tretā, and the Dvāpara *yuga*. They are different again in the Kali *yuga*, in accordance with the character of the age."

[^3]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-afd7d9](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-afd7d9). "Austerity (*tapas*) is supreme in the Kṛta *yuga*; knowledge (*jñāna*) is said to be so in the Tretā. In the Dvāpara, they call it sacrifice (*yajña*); in the Kali *yuga*, only charity (*dāna*) is supreme."

[^4]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-121575](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-121575). "In the Kṛta *yuga*, the *dharmas* are those of Manu; in the Tretā, those of Gautama are remembered. In the Dvāpara, they are those of Śaṅkha and Likhita; in the Kali *yuga*, those of Parāśara are remembered."

cases.prompt: Which work argues that animals slain at a sacrifice attain higher rebirths and that 'in a sacrifice, killing is not killing,' while in the same chapter declaring that one who kills an animal needlessly will himself suffer a violent death in as many future births as the slain animal had hairs? Quote both verses and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The deliberate juxtaposition of these two verses — sacrificial slaughter as ennobling vs. wanton slaughter as multi-life retribution measured in hair-counts — is a distinctive Manu argument structure on meat-eating; the hair-count formula is a Manu signature.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Manusmṛti* (Laws of Manu), Chapter 5, presents this argument in three consecutive verses (5.38–40).**[^1][^2][^3]

It first declares that needlessly killing an animal brings retribution matching the number of its hairs:

> As many hairs as the animal has, so many times does the one who kills it for no reason suffer a violent death in future births.[^1]

It then justifies ritual killing in sacrifices (explicitly stating "in a sacrifice, killing is not killing") and the higher rebirths for the slain:

> Animals were created for the sake of sacrifice by the Self-existent One himself; the sacrifice is for the prosperity of this whole world; therefore, **in a sacrifice, killing is not killing**.[^2]  
> Herbs, animals, trees, beasts, and birds, **which have met their death for the sake of a sacrifice, attain higher states of existence again**.[^3]

These verses contrast permissible sacrificial killing (benefiting both sacrificer and victim) with needless slaughter (which incurs karmic punishment).

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 5, paragraph p-42b5e1](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-42b5e1).

[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 5, paragraph p-d81302](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-d81302).

[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 5, paragraph p-aa01df](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-aa01df).

cases.prompt: In the corpus, which text argues that even a matter seen with one's own eyes still requires careful examination — illustrating the point with the paired claim that 'the sky appears like a surface, a firefly like a fire,' yet there is in fact no surface in the sky and no fire in a firefly? Quote the verse and give the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The sky-surface and firefly-fire double-illusion analogy, used specifically as a warning about the unreliability of direct perception in legal proof, occurs only in this work and not in any of the other Sanskrit smṛtis or Indic texts in the corpus.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Nāradasmṛti**, Chapter 1 (paragraphs from *Mātṛkā* 1 on legal procedure), argues that even self-evident matters require scrutiny due to deceptive appearances. It states: "Untrue things appear true, and true things appear untrue; various states of affairs are seen. Therefore, examination is proper."[^1] It illustrates this with the verse: "The sky appears like a surface, a firefly like a fire. But there is no surface in the sky, nor is there fire in a firefly." (Nar. M1.63)[^2] The text concludes: "Therefore, even a matter seen with one's own eyes should be properly examined. By making matters known after examination, one does not deviate from Dharma."[^3]

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**No work in the Falsafa corpus declares witnesses to be of exactly eleven kinds, with the specified division into five appointed kinds (written witness, reminded witness, accidental witness, secret witness, and subsequent witness) and six unappointed kinds.**

The closest reference appears in the *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1 (specifically verses Brh_1,5.4–5), which lists **twelve kinds** of witnesses: a subscribed witness, recorded witness, concealed witness, reminded witness, kinsman, messenger, chance witness, secondary witness, one present at the transaction, king, chief judge, and village.[^1]

Searches across the corpus for distinctive phrases (e.g., "witnesses eleven kinds", "five appointed", "secret witness", "subsequent witness") and related terms (e.g., "witnesses", "kinds of witnesses") yielded no matches for eleven kinds or the exact categorization and verse requested. Other mentions of witnesses occur (e.g., in *Āṅgirasa Smṛti* Chapter 1 and *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* throughout Chapter 1), but none align with the query.[^2]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a stanzaic catalogue of misfortunes — Weland bound by Niðhad's sinew-fetters, Beadohild's unwilling pregnancy, Mæðhild and Geat, Theodric's thirty winters in the Mærings' stronghold, the wolf-minded Eormanric ruling the Goths — each stanza closing with the same one-line refrain of consolation, before the speaker finally names himself as a scop displaced from his lord's favor by a rival named Heorrenda? Quote at least three occurrences of the refrain together with the speaker's self-naming, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The combination of (a) a stanzaic structure with an identical recurring refrain, (b) this exact catalogue of Germanic-legend exempla (Weland/Beadohild/Mæðhild/Theodric/Eormanric), and (c) a first-person scop dispossessed by Heorrenda is unique to this single chapter. Cynewulf's Andreas/Elene/Juliana are continuous Christian-saint narratives without refrains or pre-Christian legendary catalogues; no other corpus work uses Weland, Beadohild, or Heorrenda.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies* (slug: `unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e`), chapter 13 ("Welund him be wurman wraeces cunnade"), which contains the Old English poem known as *Deor*.**[^1]

This chapter features a stanzaic catalogue of legendary misfortunes—Weland (Wayland the Smith) bound by Niðhad's sinew-fetters,[^2] Beadohild's unwilling pregnancy,[^3] the boundless sorrow-love between Mæðhild and Geat,[^4] Theodric's thirty winters in the Mærings' stronghold,[^5] and Eormanric's wolfish rule over the Goths[^6]—with each stanza closing on the refrain **"That passed over; so may this."** The speaker, a scop (poet) displaced from his lord's favor by a rival named Heorrenda, finally names himself **"Deor"** in the closing stanza.[^7]

Three occurrences of the refrain (from the Weland, Mæðhilde, and Deor stanzas):

> Weland knew well the worm’s cruel bite,  
> a resolute earl endured his hardships,  
> had for his company sorrow and longing,  
> winter-cold misery; woes he often met  
> after Niðhad had laid heavy needs on him,  
> supple sinew-bonds on a better man.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^2]

> We have heard tell of Mæðhilde's plight,  
> how the love of Geat grew boundless,  
> so that sorrow-love stole all their sleep.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^4]

> This I can say about my own self,  
> that for a time I was the Heodenings' scop,  
> dear to my lord. **Deor was my name.**  
> For many winters I had a goodly following,  
> a loyal lord, until Heorrenda now,  
> that song-crafty man, received the land-right  
> which the protector of earls had granted me before.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^7]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [full chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-1308a0).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-d11ebc).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-5821a4).

[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-ac23ab).

[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-160f1c).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus contemplates a shattered city as the 'work of giants', describing roofs toppled and a barred gate plundered, and then turns specifically to the city's many bath-houses — stone courts where 'a stream threw up heat in a wide surge', a wall enclosing all 'within its bright embrace' where the baths were 'hot at their heart', with hot streams poured 'over the grey stone' until 'the round pool grew hot'? Quote the bath passage and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: A meditation on a ruined city that pivots into a description of working hot-spring baths with a round heated pool inside enclosing walls is unique to this single chapter (a description widely identified as Roman Aquae Sulis). Cynewulf's Christian narratives contain no such ruin-meditation; the 'wind-blown walls' passage in The Wanderer (also in this corpus work) ends at the wall, with no baths, hot streams, or round pool. No other corpus work describes hypocaust-style heated baths.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies*, Chapter 16 ("Wraetlic is thes wealstan, wyrde yebraecon; burgstede burston, brosnath enta yeweorc. Hrofas" — more commonly known as "The Ruin").**[^1]

This Old English elegy contemplates the ruins of a once-great (Roman) city, described as the "work of giants," with "roofs [...] toppled" and "the barred gate plundered."[^2] It then evokes the city's former splendor, including its many bath-houses, before turning to the detailed bath passage:

> Stone courts stood; a stream threw up heat  
> in a wide surge; a wall enclosed all  
> within its bright embrace, where the baths were,  
> hot at their heart. That was a welcome thing.  
> They let pour then over the grey stone  
> hot streams [...]  
> [...] until the round pool grew hot [...][^3]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a brief, riddle-like first-person lament in which the speaker says her people 'will devour him, should he dare to come in a raiding troop', insists 'our fates are not the same', describes her beloved as on one island while she is on another 'fast' island 'by fen surrounded', then suddenly turns to address a second man by name, telling him that 'our wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods'? Quote the address to the named second man and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The trio of (a) two named figures — one called only as a wolf-named beloved across a fen-bound island, the other an addressee named Eadwacer — (b) the cryptic 'wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods' line, and (c) the refrain 'our fates are not the same' is unique to this single chapter. No Cynewulfian saint narrative has a riddling triangle of named lovers, and no other corpus work names Eadwacer or contains the whelp-borne-by-wolf image.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies*, Chapter 4 ("Leodum is minum swylce him mon lac gife;").**[^1]

This chapter is the poem known as *Wulf and Eadwacer*, a brief Old English riddle-like elegy spoken by a woman lamenting her separation from Wulf (her beloved, on an island stalked by violent men), while addressing Eadwacer (likely the father of her child). It matches all details: her people's threat to "devour him, should he dare to come in a raiding troop"; "Our fates are not the same"; Wulf on one island, her on a "fast" one "by fen surrounded"; and the sudden address to Eadwacer about their child.[^2]

The requested quote—"Do you hear, Eadwacer? Our wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods"—is:[^3]

> Do you hear, Eadwacer? Our wretched whelp  
> a wolf bears to the woods.

cases.rationale: This pair of verses pinning the punishment for turning away an *atithi* to the precise figures of fifteen years of ancestral refusal and the 'thousand loads of wood and hundred pots of clarified butter' useless-fire-offering image is unique to this work and absent from every other smṛti, dharmaśāstra digest, and corpus text.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Parāśara Smṛti*, Chapter 1, contains the warning in verses Par_1.45–46:**

> The one from whose house an *atithi* turns away with his hopes shattered—his ancestors will not eat his offerings for fifteen years.[^1]  
> For him from whose house an *atithi* turns away with hopes shattered, his fire offering is as useless as a thousand loads of wood and a hundred pots of clarified butter.[^2]

These verses emphasize the grave consequences for a householder who fails to honor a guest (*atithi*), who arrives during the *Vaiśvadeva* ritual.

cases.rationale: The four-stage age-by-name scheme (Gaurī at 8 / Rohiṇī at 9 / Kanyā at 10 / *rajasvalā* thereafter) coupled with the lurid twelfth-year ancestors-drinking-menstrual-blood penalty is distinctive to this work; no other Sanskrit smṛti, Old Javanese dharma text, or dharmaśāstra digest in the corpus contains this specific naming sequence and sanction.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The *Pārāśara Smṛti* (work slug: `unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be`), Chapter 7 ("The Purification of Things"), contains the relevant passage.**[^1]

The verses assign the names as follows:

> An eight-year-old girl is a Gaurī; a nine-year-old is a Rohiṇī.[^2]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus identifies each of the five Tathāgatas with a specific state of the practitioner's breath — naming the breath in the right nostril as Amitābha, the breath in the left as Amoghasiddhi, the breath exiting both at once as Ratnasambhava, the breath not exiting from either side as Akṣobhya, and the culmination of the breath as Vairocana — and calls these five 'the fivefold culmination of stainless purity' or 'the Five Sacred Tastes,' locating their abodes at the tip of the nose, the forehead, and the crown of the head? Quote the five-line breath-to-Tathāgata mapping verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The pranayama-based assignment of the five Tathāgatas to right-breath / left-breath / both-breaths / no-breath / breath-culmination, capped with the label 'Five Sacred Tastes' and the three nasal-cranial abodes, is unique to this Old Javanese Mahāyāna manual; the other Old Javanese Buddhist works (Kuñjarakarṇa, Kalpabuddha, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna) and the Saiva texts (Vrhaspatitattva, Gaṇapatitattva) do not contain this specific breath-cycle catalogue of the Pañcatathāgata.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *San Hyan Kamahayanikan* (author unknown), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter maps the five Tathāgatas to specific states of the practitioner's breath as follows:

> Now, pay close attention to this if you wish to find liberation.  
> * The breath in the right is called Amitābha.  
> * The breath in the left is called Amoghasiddhi.  
> * The breath exiting both at once is called Ratnasambhava.  
> * The breath not exiting from the left or right is called Akṣobhya.  
> * The culmination of the breath is called Vairocana.  
> Their abodes are at the tip of the nose, on the forehead, and at the crown of the head. They are the fivefold culmination of stainless purity. They are called the Five Sacred Tastes.[^1]

[^1]: *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19/01/translation/#p-cf2520).

cases.rationale: This precise pairing of nostril (right vs left) with element-maṇḍala (fire / Mahendra / wind / water), each with a specific shape, color, central symbol (trident, five-pronged vajra, banner+hook+ring, crystal), and specific siddhi outcome (sarvakarma, life-and-wealth increase, ākarṣaṇa-sthāmbana-uccāraṇa, śāntika), is unique to this Old Javanese Mahāyāna manual; no other Old Javanese Buddhist or Saiva work in the corpus, and none of the Sanskrit smṛtis or non-Indic works, contains this specific breath-element-maṇḍala-siddhi correspondence.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work *San Hyan Kamahayanikan* (unknown author), Chapter 1, teaches this yogic ritual catalogue of breath maṇḍalas.**[^1]

This occurs in a section on the Seven Samādhis, after instructions on breath control (*prāṇāyāma*) and recollection of the Buddha (*buddhānusmāraṇa*). The breath in the right nostril (noted simply as "the breath in the right," implying the right nostril in this context of nāḍī practices) is visualized as:

> Now, if you desire to engage in ritual action, the breath in the right becomes a fire-maṇḍala, triangular in shape, blazing and red in color. In its center is a trident. This is your means of accomplishment for all actions.[^2]

Alternatively:

> Alternatively, make the breath in the right a Mahendra-maṇḍala, square-shaped, blazing and yellow like gold in color. In its center is an open five-pronged *vajra*. This is your means of accomplishment for the increase of life and the increase of wealth.[^3]

The breath in the left nostril becomes:

> The breath in the left becomes a formless wind-maṇḍala, black, green, or yellow in color. It blazes at both ends. Its symbol is a banner or flag. The left hand grasps a hook and a ring. This is your means of accomplishment for attraction (*ākarṣaṇa*), immobilization (*sthāmbana*), and expulsion (*uccāraṇa*).

Alternatively, as the water-maṇḍala:

> Alternatively, make the breath in the left a water-maṇḍala (*bāruṇamaṇḍala*), circular, blazing, and white in color. In its center is a subtle, small maṇḍala, like pure crystal in the middle is its form and its symbol. This is your means of accomplishment for pacification (*śāntika*).

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus that contains a brief debate about the size of the gall bladder in which a 'great fool' is paraphrased as arguing that, although it is said to be a thumb's breadth in size, the gall bladder is in fact 'as large as the seven islands' — a statement the text frames as 'the argument of a pandit.' cases.rationale: The gall bladder = Lord Īśvara identification, paired with a satirical 'fool/pandit' debate measuring it against the seven islands (sapta-dvīpa) of cosmography, is unique to this Old Javanese text. grok-4.1-fast.answer:
The passage appears in *San Hyan Mahajnana*, Chapter 1.[^1]

The teacher first describes the gall bladder as thumb-sized:[^2]  
> Now, as for the gall bladder, its measure is that of a thumb. The power of the Lord Īśvara is like crystal. The body is like a divine abode. Such is the Lord Īśvara. Contemplate this, my son, revered Kumāra.

This is juxtaposed with verse 58, paraphrasing the 'fool':[^3]  
> *Let the fool speak thus, [...] the gall bladder [...]. Of the measure of the seven islands, the king becomes powerful. // 58*

The prose gloss then frames this as the 'argument of a pandit' by a 'great fool':[^4]  
> Where is this great fool, who argues about its nature? It is said that the gall bladder is a thumb’s breadth in size. The gall bladder is as large as the seven islands. What are the seven islands? Such is the Lord Īśvara; He is of great power, all-pervading. Thus are the words of the fool. This is what is called the argument of a pandit.

cases.prompt: Find the work in the corpus that compares the Self trapped in worldly suffering to a serpent submerged in terrifying waters, and identifies the sacred syllable oṃ as the Garuḍa that swoops down to lift the serpent-Self up to the abode of Shiva. Quote the verse that names the puruṣa as nāga and oṃkāra as Garuḍa, and the prose line that says oṃkāra brings the Self to śivapada. Identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The specific mythological transposition — puruṣa-as-nāga drowning in saṃsāra, oṃkāra-as-Garuḍa rescuing it — is a distinctive image in this work and does not appear in the other Old Javanese tattva texts (Tattvajñāna, Vrhaspatitattva, Gaṇapatitattva) or in the Buddhist Kamahāyānikan / Kuñjarakarṇa narratives. The Garuḍa-rescues-nāga frame is being reused to allegorize the relationship between oṃkāra and the Self, which is an idiosyncratic move particular to this text.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *San Hyan Mahajnana* (unknown author; Kawi original, English translation), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Shaiva tantric text compares **puruṣa** (the Self or pure consciousness, trapped in the ocean of *saṃsāra* or worldly suffering) to a **nāga** (serpent) terrified in the midst of dangerous waters.[^2] The sacred syllable **oṃkāra** is identified as **Garuḍa** (the mythical bird and enemy of serpents), which swoops down to rescue it, lifting the serpent-Self upward to **śivapada** (the abode of Shiva).[^3]

The verse is:  
*In the terrible ocean of saṃsāra, puruṣa is situated like a nāga. Knowing oṃkāra to be Garuḍa [...] for the effort [...] // 64*[^4]

The key prose explication follows immediately:  
"The likeness of the revered *puruṣa* is one who is in the middle of the waters, terrified like a serpent. The holy *oṃkāra* is named Garuḍa. He is the one who brings the revered *puruṣa* to *śivapada*."[^5]

A nearby prose line reinforces *oṃkāra*'s role: "In the heart, there is what is called *śivapada* (the abode of Shiva). The holy *oṃkāra* is the supreme void, very subtle."[^6]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus maps the human body onto the seven mountains, seven oceans, and seven continents with specific organ-by-organ identifications — equating the kidneys with Mount Mālyavān, the heart with Mount Mahāmeru, the liver with Mount Vindhya, the spleen with Mount Gandhamādana; and equating urine with the fresh-water ocean, blood with the ocean of sugarcane juice, sweat with the salt ocean, fat with the oil ocean, saliva with the honey ocean, marrow with the milk ocean, and the brain with the ocean of curds; while bone is Jambudvīpa, sinew is Kuśadvīpa, flesh is Śaṅkhadvīpa, skin is Śālmalidvīpa, and teeth are Krauñcadvīpa? Quote the equations of the seven oceans and seven continents verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This organ-by-organ correspondence with the saptaparvata, saptārṇava, and saptadvīpa — including the highly specific 'brain = ocean of curds,' 'blood = ocean of sugarcane juice,' and 'sinew = Kuśadvīpa' identifications — is unique to this Old Javanese Saiva-tattva text; Vrhaspatitattva and Gaṇapatitattva enumerate the same Saptadvīpa and tattva systems but never make these body-microcosm equations, and no Sanskrit smṛti or other corpus work pairs Mount Mālyavān with the kidneys or the brain with curds.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**San Hyan Tattvajnana, Chapter 1.**

This work maps the human body onto the seven mountains (*Saptaparvvata*), seven oceans (*Saptārṇṇava*), and seven continents (*Saptadvīpa*) of the cosmos as follows (among other correspondences in the body-universe, or *bhuvanaśarīra*):[^1]

The seven mountains are: the kidneys as Mount Mālyavān, the pelvic region as Mount Niṣedha, the spleen as Mount Gandhamādana, the lungs as Mount Malayamahīdhara, the gall bladder as Mount Triśṛṅga, the liver as Mount Vindhya, and the heart as Mount Mahāmeru.

**The seven oceans (*Saptārṇṇava*) are as follows:**  
* Urine is the fresh water ocean.  
* Blood is the ocean of sugarcane juice.  
* Sweat is the salt ocean.  
* Fat is the oil ocean.  
* Saliva is the honey ocean.  
* Marrow is the milk ocean.  
* The brain is the ocean of curds.[^2]  
**Such is what is called the seven oceans.**

**The seven continents (*Saptadvīpa*) are as follows:**  
* Bone is Jambudvīpa.  
* Sinew is Kuśadvīpa.  
* Flesh is Śaṅkhadvīpa.  
* Skin is Śālmalidvīpa.  
* The neck is Gomedhadvīpa.  
* Bodily vapor is Puṣkaradvīpa.  
* Teeth are Krauñcadvīpa.[^3]

[^1]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/).
[^2]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-417b1b).
[^3]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-09a726).

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus describes a graded fourteen-step cosmic devolution in which a being, 'lacking in yoga,' falls down a fixed ladder of births — Tripuruṣa becoming Pañcaṛṣi, Pañcaṛṣi becoming Saptaṛṣi, Saptaṛṣi becoming Devaṛṣi, then Devatā, Vidyādhara, Gandharva, Dānava, Daitya, Rākṣasa, Bhūta Yakṣa, Bhūta Dĕṅĕn, Bhūta Kāla, Bhūta Piśāca, finally human, and at last a being of the triyak realm — and immediately afterwards classifies the five kinds of triyak as pāśu (village domestic animal), mṛga (forest wild beast), pakṣī (bird), mīna (fish in water), and pipīlikā (the belly-mover whose 'other names are not known')? Quote the descent ladder verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: This precise stepwise descent through fourteen named ranks of beings, each transition explicitly captioned 'if lacking in yoga,' culminating in the five-fold triyak classification with pipīlikā as the unnameable belly-mover, is unique to this Old Javanese Tattvajñāna text; Vrhaspatitattva enumerates similar Saiva tattvas and discusses Daityas and Rākṣasas but never strings them into this specific yoga-failure ladder, and no Sanskrit corpus text (Manu, Yājñavalkya, the smṛtis, Vīramitrodaya) lists Bhūta Dĕṅĕn or this ordered devolution.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is *San Hyan Tattvajnana* (Unknown author), Chapter 1.**[^1]

The graded fourteen-step cosmic devolution is described as follows (quoted verbatim):

> Now, remember again the form of the Divine *Ātmā*'s descent, a coarse and doubtful entity. The *Tripuruṣa*—the *Tripuruṣa* are Lord Brahmā, Viṣṇu, and Īśvara; they are the primordial *sattva* beings. If that primordial *sattva* is negligent and **lacking in yoga**, it becomes *Pañcaṛṣi*. ***Pañcaṛṣi***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Saptaṛṣi*. ***Saptaṛṣi***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Devaṛṣi*. ***Devaṛṣi***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Devatā*. ***Devatā***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Vidyādhara*. ***Vidyādhara***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Gandharva*. ***Gandharva***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Dānava*. ***Dānava***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Daitya*. ***Daitya***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Rākṣasa*. ***Rākṣasa***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Bhūta Yakṣa*. ***Bhūta Yakṣa***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes ***Bhūta Dĕṅĕn***. ***Bhūta Dĕṅĕn***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Bhūta Kāla*. ***Bhūta Kāla***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes *Bhūta Piśāca*. ***Bhūta Piśāca***, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes human.[^1]

Immediately afterwards, the text classifies the five kinds of *triyak*:

> A human, if lacking in *yoga*, becomes a being of the *triyak* realm. There are five kinds of *triyak*: as a domestic animal (*pāśu*) it is born in the village; as a wild beast (*mṛga*) it is born in the forest; as a bird (*pakṣī*) it is born everywhere that flies; as a fish (*mīna*) it is born in the water; ***pipīlikā*** is the name for a being that moves on its belly. **Its other names are not known**.[^1]

[^1]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-357c74).

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**San Hyan Tattvajnana, Chapter 1.**

The assignments of patron divinities (*deva*) to three classes of blades (*asi*) by social function appear in the context of describing *buddhi-rajas* (intellect dominated by passion), where the *Rākṣasa*, *Daitya*, and *Dānava* are linked to blades used by kings, farmers, and priests, respectively.[^1] This immediately precedes further elaboration on *buddhi-tamas*, but the text soon frames the distinction among these blade classes within a blacksmith simile to illustrate how tools (tongs, hammer, anvil, file) and raw iron submit entirely to the smith's will, while only the finished blade enacts good and evil.[^2]

The three blade-divinity assignments are quoted verbatim as follows:

> Now, the reality of this is that the *Rākṣasa* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the weapons of a king. All kinds of blades that are currently used to take life have the *Rākṣasa* as their deity.[^1]

> The *Daitya* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the implements of farmers, such as a *luke* or a *pṛkul* (types of agricultural blades). All kinds of blades used as a means to produce a livelihood have the *Daitya* as their deity.[^1]

> The *Dānava* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the implements of a priest, such as a *pamiso*, *kartrī*, or *karayu* (types of ritual knives). The *Dānava* is their deity.[^1]

cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus illustrates the doctrine that virtues and vices arise from association by telling of two parrots named Gāvākṣa and Girikā — one raised by a hunter and the other by a scholar — where a king who has lost his retinue is first brought to the hunter's house and the parrot Girikā cries out 'Come on, come on! Smash his head!' while the scholar's parrot Gāvākṣa instead welcomes the king with words of greeting, offers him a new mat to sit on, betel nut and fresh betel leaf with uncooked lime to chew, and invites him to bathe in the spring? Quote the parrots' two contrasting speeches verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The named pair of parrots Gāvākṣa and Girikā, the explicit hunter-vs-scholar upbringing contrast, the king-separated-from-retinue frame, and the two specific quoted speeches (the violent 'Smash his head!' from Girikā vs Gāvākṣa's hospitable betel-nut-and-spring-bath welcome) form a single named exemplum that is unique to this Old Javanese verse compendium's bilingual gloss; no other corpus work — neither the Sanskrit smṛtis, the Vīramitrodaya digest, the Old English religious poems, the Urdu dīwāns, the French treatises, nor any other Old Javanese text in the corpus — contains this two-parrots-and-the-lost-king narrative with these proper names.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The work is the *Slokāntara* (author unknown), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter illustrates the doctrine that "flaws and virtues arise from association" (Sanskrit śloka 45) through the story of two parrots: **Gāvākṣa**, raised by a scholar (in a hermitage), and **Girikā**, raised by a hunter. A king, separated from his retinue while hunting, arrives first at the hunter's house, where Girikā cries out:

> "Come on, come on! Smash his head!"[^1]

Frightened, the king flees to the scholar's hermitage, where Gāvākṣa welcomes him with refined hospitality:

> "Oh, fortunate are you, O King! My lord has deigned to visit the hermitage. Pray, rest, sir. Sit upon the new mat. Chew this betel nut and fresh betel leaf, with uncooked lime. If my lord is weary, pray, bathe, Your Majesty, in the spring."[^1]
