cases.prompt: Which work in the corpus opens with a deity who, after rescuing the Earth from the cosmic waters in the form of a Boar whose tusks are the sacrificial post and tongue is the sacrificial fire, vanishes — prompting the Earth-goddess to take the form of a beautiful woman with lips red as the Bandhujīva flower, seek out the sage Kashyapa, and at his direction travel to the Ocean of Milk to ask the reclining god 'what will be my support?' before being seated on a golden throne to receive the law? **Viṣṇu Smṛti (Vishnu Smriti), Chapter 1** opens with this exact narrative in its introductory verses.[^1]

Vishnu incarnates as a cosmic Boar to rescue the Earth from the waters of dissolution: "Took on the auspicious form of the Boar... And lifted up the Earth."[^2] The Boar's form symbolically embodies the Vedic sacrifice, "whose tusks were the sacrificial post... Whose tongue was the sacrificial fire."[^3]

After recreating the world and vanishing to "a destination then unknown to the worlds,"[^4] the Earth (Vasudhā or Dhara) ponders her support: "What will be my support now?" She resolves to consult the sage Kashyapa.[^5]

Taking "the form of a woman," with "lips auspicious and red like the Bandhujīva flower," she approaches him.[^6] Kashyapa directs her to Vishnu in the "Ocean of Milk," where she finds him "resting upon the couch of Śeṣa" (the reclining god).[^7]

There, she asks again: "Now, therefore, O Lord of gods, what will be my support?" Vishnu replies that good people devoted to *varṇas* and *āśramas* will support her, then seats her "on this excellent golden throne" to expound the law (*dharma*): "Be seated, O you of beautiful thighs, on this excellent golden throne. Seated comfortably, hear now the laws as I declare them to you."[^8]

cases.prompt: Find the legal-procedure chapter in the corpus that names exactly five ordeals — the balance, fire, water, poison, and the sacred libation — and then prescribes their procedural details with these specific numbers: an iron ball of fifty palas placed in the accused's hands, who must walk through seven circles each of sixteen finger-breadths, with seven Aśvattha leaves bound on his hands; for the poison ordeal the accused must eat 'the Śārṅga poison from the Himalayan mountains'; and for the sacred-libation ordeal the accused is pure if no calamity befalls him within fourteen days. Nārada-smṛti, Bṛhaspati-smṛti, and Kātyāyana-smṛti also discuss ordeals, but none combines this specific set of procedural numbers with the maternal address to the Balance, and Manu only mentions ordeals in passing without these details.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**The chapter matching this description is Chapter 1 (the sole chapter) of the *Nārada Smṛti* (Nārada's Treatise on Legal Procedure), in its section on ordeals (20.6–20.48).**[^1]

This text names exactly these **five ordeals**: the balance (*dhaṭa*), fire (*agni*), water (*udaka*), poison (*viṣa*), and consecrated water or sacred libation (*kośa*).[^2] It provides their procedural details, including:

- **Fire ordeal**: The accused, having fasted and wrapped seven Aśvattha leaves around his hands with thread, takes "a ball of iron heated to fire, weighing fifty *palas*," and walks "slowly for seven steps," each separated by circles spaced thirty-two finger-breadths (i.e., seven circles each of sixteen finger-breadths).[^3]
- **Poison ordeal**: Seven grains (one-twentieth of a sixth of a *pala*, less an eighth) of poison "from the Śṛṅga or Himavat mountains" (i.e., Śārṅga poison from the Himalayan mountains), mixed with ghee.[^4]
- **Sacred libation (consecrated water) ordeal**: The accused drinks it; he is pure unless "any personal affliction... [occurs] within a week, or again within two weeks" (i.e., no calamity within fourteen days).[^5]

The verse addressing the balance as **"O Mother"** (praying to be raised if pure, lowered if sinful) is:

> You are called 'Balance' (*dhaṭa*) by words that are synonyms for *Dharma*. You know the sins and meritorious deeds of all beings. You alone, O Balance, know what mortals do not.[^6]

cases.prompt: Find the ghazal in the corpus whose every couplet ends on the same short refrain 'is love' (radif: '-aar ka' in the original), in which one couplet declares that even the heart's fire will not be extinguished beneath the dust of the grave — and that therefore a tall chinar tree will grow upon the speaker's tomb. The same ghazal contains a couplet calling the bittersweet juice of teardrops 'the sherbet of the pomegranate of the highest paradise (khuld-e-bareen),' and closes with the poet's signature warning that this tavern has no place for the wise, so one should flee the world. Quote the chinar-on-the-grave couplet and the pomegranate-of-paradise couplet verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
cases.rationale: The named species 'chinar' growing on the speaker's grave (a Kashmiri-tree image, not a generic 'tree' or 'cypress') is absent from Ghalib's Diwan and from every other work in the corpus. Pairing it with the very specific 'sherbet of the pomegranate of khuld-e-bareen' from the same ghazal, both within a radif-'is love' refrain, fixes the chapter uniquely.

grok-4.1-fast.answer:
**Diwan-E-Zauq** by Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, chapter 56 ("hungaama garm hasti-e-na-paaidaar ka").[^1]

This ghazal's every couplet ends with the refrain "is love," matching the radif '-aar ka' in the original Urdu.[^1] The chinar-on-the-grave couplet is:

> My heart's own fire, in the grave, will glow, is love.  
> A tall Chinar upon my tomb will grow, is love.[^2]

The bittersweet-teardrops couplet, referring to their juice as that of "Eden's pomegranate" (rendering *khuld-e-bareen*, the pomegranate of the highest paradise), is:

> You ask what sweetness bitter teardrops show, is love?  
> The juice of Eden's pomegranate in its flow, is love.[^3]

The ghazal closes with the poet's *takhallus* (signature): "O Zauq, if you are wise, then from this world just go, is love. / This tavern has no place for those who think they know, is love."[^1]
